sentence: Although the weather was terrible we decided to go hiking
explanation: <Although> introduces a contrast: despite the bad weather, they went hiking.

sentence: Not only did she pass the exam but she also got the highest mark
hint: Not only + inversion, but also + clause
explanation: <Not only ... but also> requires inversion in the first clause.

sentence: Had I known about the delay I would have taken a different route
hint: Had + subject + past participle, would have + past participle
explanation: Inverted third conditional: <Had I known> = <If I had known>.

sentence: The project was completed on time despite the many challenges we faced
explanation: <Despite> is followed by a noun phrase, not a clause with a verb.

sentence: No sooner had we arrived than it started to rain heavily
hint: No sooner + had + subject + past participle + than
explanation: <No sooner ... than> requires inversion and means 'immediately after'.

sentence: Whereas some people prefer city life others enjoy living in the countryside
explanation: <Whereas> introduces a contrast between two different preferences.

sentence: The more you practise the better you will become at speaking English
hint: The more + clause, the better + clause
explanation: <The more ... the better> is a comparative correlative structure.

sentence: She not only speaks French fluently but also has a good command of German
hint: Subject + not only + verb, but also + verb
explanation: <Not only ... but also> without inversion when the subject comes first.

sentence: However hard she tried she could not solve the problem
Neither the manager nor the employees were aware of the changes
explanation: <Neither ... nor> connects two negative elements. The verb agrees with the nearest subject.

sentence: Only after reading the report did he understand the full extent of the problem
explanation: <Only after> at the start requires inversion: <did he understand>.

sentence: Much as I appreciate your help I think I can manage on my own
explanation: <Much as> means 'although I very much' — it introduces a concession.
